Executive Summary

  • Slovak firms reported a net easing in bank loan interest rates, contrasting sharply with tightening across the euro area and Germany, yet non-interest financing costs tightened markedly and available loan sizes declined slightly.
  • Financing gaps improved across products, with bank loan stress now lower than in the euro area, but trade credit stress persisted and remained above euro-area levels despite some narrowing.
  • Access to bank credit improved substantially, with rejection and discouragement rates falling and the overall access gap dropping below the euro-area average, though application rates halved, indicating weaker demand for bank loans.
  • Firms’ expectations for future bank loan availability turned less negative but remained adverse, while trade credit availability expectations deteriorated further, exceeding euro-area pessimism.
  • Slovak firms cited working capital financing most often, used fixed investment in line with the euro area, and sharply increased financing for hiring and training, yet turnover and profits continued to decline and labour costs rose at the fastest pace recorded.
  • Business sentiment weakened: expected turnover growth eased slightly below the euro-area average and fixed investment expectations fell sharply, nearly converging with the euro area after previously outpacing it.

Financing Conditions

Slovak firms report net easing in interest rates, bucking EA trend

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net -8.3% of Slovak firms (n=48) reported easing in the level of interest rates, contrasting sharply with net tightening of +11.1% across the EA and +28.4% in DE.
  • A net 42.4% of Slovak firms (n=48) reported tightening in non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions), a marked deterioration from n/a in wave 36, and well above the EA net of +28.4%.
  • A net 7.0% of Slovak firms (n=48) reported tightening in available loan size, reversing the net easing of -7.9% in the previous wave, and slightly above the EA net of +3.7%.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak bank loan conditions eased sharply while trade credit stress persisted

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • For bank loans, a net -7.6pp gap (n=240) signals SK firms are now LESS stressed than EA (+3.7pp gap), as need fell sharply to -6.2pp and availability turned positive (+1.4pp), compared with a gap of +10.5pp in the previous wave when SK was MORE stressed than EA.
  • While bank loan conditions eased markedly, the credit line gap remained at +4.8pp (n=256) — still demand-driven (need +3.1pp, availability -1.7pp) — though narrowing from +13.1pp in wave 36, leaving SK slightly LESS stressed than EA (+6.6pp gap).
  • Trade credit stress persisted with a net gap of +9.8pp (n=176), leaving SK MORE stressed than EA (+6.8pp gap), as need stayed elevated at +10.1pp while availability remained near flat (+0.3pp), compared with a gap of +16.4pp in wave 36.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak financing gap nearly halved, falling below euro-area average in wave 37

Bank Loan Applications and Access Conditions (Q7A/Q7B)

  • A net 3.58% of Slovak firms (n=147) faced a bank credit access gap in wave 37, a sharp improvement from 7.38% in wave 36 and now below the EA average of 5.02% (n=3,406).
  • The improvement was driven by both components: Slovak rejection rates fell from 8.78% to 5.31% and discouragement rates eased from 3.33% to 2.34%, signalling reduced supply-side tightening and less self-censorship in credit demand.
  • Slovak application rates dropped markedly from 46.16% to 23.51%, suggesting fewer firms sought bank loans outright, which may partly explain the lower observed gap rather than easier credit conditions alone.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms' bank loan availability expectations improve sharply but remain negative

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net -1.93% of Slovak firms (n=125) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate in wave 37, a marked improvement compared with a net -12.62% in the previous wave.
  • Despite this recovery, Slovak firms' forward expectations for bank loans remained negative while EA firms shifted to a net -0.45% (n=2688) in wave 37, down from +0.82% in wave 36, suggesting persistent local supply tightness as EA sentiment also turned slightly adverse.
  • Slovak firms reported the sharpest deterioration in trade credit availability expectations at a net -11.68% (n=89), far exceeding the EA net of -0.52% (n=1875) and improving only modestly from -18.98% in the prior wave, signalling continued stress in non-bank short-term financing.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms stand out for heavy use of financing for working capital

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • A net 55.8% of Slovak firms (n=237) cited inventory and working capital as a financing purpose, well above the EA average of 38.8%, compared with 53.2% in the previous wave.
  • A net 40.3% of Slovak firms (n=237) used financing for fixed investment, closely in line with the EA (39.4%) but notably below Germany (49.0%), edging up from 39.1% in wave 36.
  • Slovak firms' use of financing for hiring and training (26.9%, n=237) rose sharply from 19.4% in the prior wave, yet remained broadly aligned with the EA average of 27.4%.

Slovak banks' willingness to lend surges, outpacing euro-area peers

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net +10.4% of Slovak firms (n=300) reported improved willingness of banks to provide credit in wave 37, a sharp reversal from a net -2.4% in the previous wave, and above the EA net of +5.7% (n=6,036).
  • A net -38.3% of Slovak firms (n=460) reported a deterioration in the general economic outlook, signalling a persistent macro-level drag, though markedly less negative than the prior wave's net -51.2% (n=268).
  • A net -25.0% of Slovak firms (n=328) reported deteriorating access to public financial support, an improvement from -33.9% in wave 36 (n=212), yet still worse than the EA net of -15.3% (n=6,870).

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak firms report falling turnover as cost pressures outpace euro-area peers

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net -14.62% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported declining turnover in wave 37, broadly unchanged from -15.16% in the previous quarter, and far below the EA net balance of +5.91%.
  • A net -22.43% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported falling profits, an improvement from -37.52% in the prior wave, yet still markedly worse than the EA net balance of -10.37%.
  • A net 61.84% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported rising labour costs, exceeding both the EA average of +50.23% and last wave's Slovak reading of +53.74%, signalling intensifying cost pressures.

Slovak fixed investment outlook collapsed while EA remained broadly stable

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 20.9% of Slovak firms (n=169) reported expecting higher turnover, down from a net 24.2% in the previous wave, remaining slightly below the EA average of 23.2% (n=5,923).
  • A net 10.8% of Slovak firms (n=169) expected fixed investment to increase, a sharp decline of nearly 15 percentage points compared with a net 25.7% in the previous wave.
  • Slovak investment outlook (+10.8%) nearly converged with the EA average (+12.6%), whereas in the prior wave Slovakia had significantly outpaced the euro area (25.7% vs 13.9%), signalling a pronounced reversal in capex sentiment.

Slovak firms expect input prices to surge, squeezing selling-price margins

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • A mean expected selling-price increase of +4.63% was reported by Slovak firms (n=226), well above the EA average of +2.77% and up sharply from +3.41% in the previous wave.
  • Slovak firms anticipate +8.37% input price rises alongside +5.49% wage growth (n=233), compressing margins; the current labour cost net balance stands at a very elevated +61.84%, the highest of any business-situation item for Slovakia in wave 37.
  • Short-run inflation expectations among Slovak firms rose to a mean of +4.79% (n=211) for the 12-month horizon, up from +4.34% in wave 36, and significantly above the EA mean of +3.36%, signalling persistently above-average domestic cost pressure.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms see inflation risks skewed sharply to the upside, outpacing euro-area peers

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 53% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported upside inflation risks in wave 37, well above the euro-area net balance of +45% (n=5,067).
  • Slovak firms' upside risk balance eased modestly from a net +60% in wave 36 (n=134), while the EA rose from +41%, narrowing the gap.
  • Corroborating local price persistence, Slovak firms' average selling-price expectation (mean 4.63) was materially higher than the EA (2.77) and DE (2.44) in wave 37.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms rate access to finance as least pressing, well below EA average

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Slovak firms scored access to finance at 3.72 out of 10 (n=233) in wave 37, notably below the EA average of 4.14, suggesting financing constraints are perceived as less acute in Slovakia than across the euro area.
  • The access to finance score for Slovak firms was virtually unchanged from wave 30 (3.73), indicating a persistently low and stable level of concern compared with EA peers, where the score edged up from 4.06 to 4.14.
  • The most pressing problem for Slovak firms in wave 37 was availability of skilled staff (6.57 out of 10, n=237), followed closely by costs of production or labour (6.34, n=237), dwarfing the access to finance score of 3.72.

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
